title: Deliberate-Language
text: 1 Preface

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.1 Why This Volume Exists
Language does not stop at describing the world.
It begins there—but it does not stay there.
After naming, describing, and organizing reality, a more demanding task emerges:

deciding what matters
choosing between alternatives
acting under uncertainty
coordinating with others

At this point, language changes its role.
It is no longer only a medium of representation.
It becomes a tool of judgment.
This volume exists to examine that transition.

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.2 From Expression to Deliberation
The earlier volumes establish a progression:

Volume I — Common Sense: what exists in the world
Volume II — Granular Language: how the world can be described precisely
Volume III — Structured Expression: how thoughts are organized into coherent form
Volume IV — Pragmatic Language: how language operates in real interaction

This volume continues that trajectory.
It moves from expression to deliberation.
Here, language is no longer evaluated by clarity alone, but by its ability to:

define problems
construct options
compare alternatives
support judgment
guide action

The question is no longer:

“Can this be said clearly?”

but:

“Can this be decided well?”

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.3 What This Volume Is About
This volume studies language as an operational system for:

judgment
choice
reasoning under uncertainty
planning and strategy
collective decision-making

It focuses on how language makes the following possible:

turning vague situations into defined problems
transforming goals into criteria
expanding or constraining the space of options
making trade-offs explicit
representing uncertainty without collapsing into confusion
moving from evidence to conclusion
turning decisions into structured plans
coordinating reasoning across multiple people

In short:
Language becomes the structure through which decisions are formed and executed.

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.4 What This Volume Is Not
This is not a book on:

formal logic
decision theory mathematics
game theory
business strategy frameworks
organizational behavior

Those fields may appear as references.
But this volume does not attempt to replace them.
Instead, it operates at a different layer:

the language layer that makes all of them usable.

The focus is not on formulas, but on how problems, options, risks, and decisions are expressed, structured, and transformed through language.

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.5 The Core Shift
A critical shift occurs in this volume:
From
describing reality
to
intervening in reality
This shift introduces new constraints:

decisions must be made without full information
alternatives compete and cannot all be chosen
consequences unfold over time
errors are costly
other people are involved

Language must now handle:

uncertainty
incompleteness
conflict
responsibility

This requires a different kind of precision.
Not descriptive precision—but decision precision.

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.6 How to Read This Volume
This is not a passive reading text.
Each part corresponds to a stage in a real decision process:
Problem → Goal → Options → Comparison → Uncertainty → Judgment → Plan → Coordination
The reader is encouraged to:

map real decisions onto this structure
test how problems change when re-framed
observe how options expand or collapse
examine where assumptions are hidden
track how judgments are formed and revised

This volume should be used as:

a diagnostic tool
a thinking scaffold
a decision framework

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.7 Language as a Decision Infrastructure
A central claim of this volume is:

Decisions do not emerge directly from facts.
They emerge from how facts are structured in language.

Poor decisions are often not failures of intelligence,
but failures of structure:

unclear problems
unstable criteria
missing alternatives
hidden assumptions
unarticulated trade-offs

Language, when properly structured, acts as an infrastructure that stabilizes these elements.
It does not guarantee correct decisions.
But it makes better decisions possible.

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.8 Toward Strategy and Collective Reasoning
The later parts of this volume extend beyond individual judgment:

decisions become plans
plans become strategies
strategies operate under uncertainty
multiple agents must coordinate

At this stage, language is no longer purely cognitive.
It becomes:

a coordination mechanism
a conflict surface
a shared reasoning medium

Understanding this transition is essential for:

leadership
collaboration
system design
large-scale decision-making

title: Deliberate-Language
text:
1.9 Final Orientation
This volume should be read with a shift in mindset:
Do not ask only:

“Is this true?”

Also ask:

“What decision does this enable?”
“What alternatives does this create or remove?”
“What assumptions does this hide?”
“What action does this lead to?”

Because at this level:
Language is not only about understanding the world.
It is about shaping what happens next.

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
text:
Orientation
Deliberate thinking does not begin with comparison or judgment.
It begins earlier.
Before any evaluation can take place, three conditions must be established:
Without these, judgment has no stable object,
and strategy has no direction.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
text:
the problem must be defined
the goal must be clarified
the space of possible options must be constructed
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
text:
The Entry Point of Deliberation
Every decision operates within a structured space.
This space is not given by reality itself.
It is constructed through language.
Different formulations of:

what the problem is
what counts as success
what alternatives exist

will produce entirely different decision paths.
A change in language at this stage is not cosmetic.
It is structural.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
text:
Three Foundational Constructions
This part focuses on three foundational constructions:

Problem
The problem defines:

what is being attended to
what is considered relevant
what is excluded

A poorly defined problem makes correct judgment impossible,
regardless of reasoning quality.



Goal
The goal defines:

what counts as success
what criteria will be used
what trade-offs are acceptable

Without explicit goals, comparison collapses into preference.



Decision Space
The decision space defines:

what options are visible
what alternatives are imaginable
what possibilities are ignored

A narrow decision space leads to false necessity.
An unstructured one leads to confusion.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
text:
Structural Dependency
These three elements are not independent.
They form a dependency structure:
Problem → Goal → Decision Space

how the problem is framed constrains the goals
how the goals are defined shapes the space of options
how options are constructed feeds back into problem understanding

Deliberation is therefore not linear.
It is an iterative refinement of these three structures.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
section: Failure Modes at the Entry Point
text:
Failure Modes at the Entry Point
Most decision failures originate here.
Common failure modes include:

treating symptoms as problems
defining goals without criteria
collapsing multiple goals into one
assuming a fixed set of options
ignoring alternative framings
failing to expand the decision space

These are not errors of logic.
They are errors of construction.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
text:
Function of This Part
This part establishes the foundation for all subsequent reasoning.
It provides the tools to:

define problems with precision
construct goals with explicit criteria
generate and structure decision spaces

Only after these are in place can comparison, trade-off, and judgment become meaningful.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
text:
Transition
Once multiple options exist within a defined decision space,
deliberation enters its next phase:
comparison, trade-off, and evaluation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.1 The Problem Before the Problem
Deliberate thinking does not begin with solving.
It begins with framing.
Before a person compares alternatives, evaluates risks, forms judgment, or chooses a strategy, something more basic has already happened: a situation has been turned into a problem.
This transformation is not automatic.
Reality presents events, pressures, symptoms, conflicts, failures, desires, and disturbances. But none of these becomes a “problem” until language gives it a form.
A problem is not merely something unpleasant.
It is not merely something difficult.
It is not merely something that demands attention.
A problem is a structured object of thought.
To frame a problem is to say:
This means that problem framing is not a neutral introduction to thinking. It is already an act of judgment.
The way a problem is stated determines what can be seen, what can be ignored, what can be blamed, what can be changed, and what kinds of solutions appear reasonable.
A poorly framed problem can make excellent reasoning useless.
A well-framed problem does not solve the issue by itself, but it gives thought a stable object to work on.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
what the issue is
where its boundary lies
what caused or shaped it
who or what is involved
what matters about it
what kind of response it calls for
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
section: 2.2 A Problem Is a Linguistic Construction
text:
2.2 A Problem Is a Linguistic Construction
In ordinary speech, people often treat problems as if they simply exist.
They say:

“The problem is money.”
“The problem is time.”
“The problem is motivation.”
“The problem is communication.”
“The problem is the market.”
“The problem is the system.”

These statements feel clear because they name something.
But naming is not yet framing.
To name a problem is to point toward an area of concern.
To frame a problem is to define its structure.
For example:

“The problem is time.”

This sentence is too compressed. It may refer to many different structures:

there is not enough time
time is being used inefficiently
priorities are unclear
tasks are overcommitted
deadlines are unrealistic
sequencing is wrong
attention is fragmented
the wrong work is being done at the wrong stage

Each formulation opens a different path of thought.
If the problem is “not enough time,” the solution may be to reduce scope.
If the problem is “poor sequencing,” the solution may be to reorder work.
If the problem is “unclear priorities,” the solution may be to define criteria.
If the problem is “fragmented attention,” the solution may be to redesign the working environment.
The same situation can generate different problems depending on how it is framed.
This is the first principle of deliberate language:

A problem is not only found in reality.
It is constructed through language.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.3 What a Frame Does
A frame is a structure that makes a situation thinkable.
It selects, organizes, and emphasizes.
When language frames a problem, it performs several operations at once.
It defines the object of concern.
It draws boundaries around the issue.
It identifies relevant causes.
It assigns agency or responsibility.
It suggests what kind of action is appropriate.
It implies what would count as improvement.
For example:

“Students are lazy.”

This frame makes student character the central object. It directs attention toward discipline, attitude, and motivation.
But consider another formulation:

“The learning environment does not produce enough feedback.”

Now the frame changes. The object is no longer student character, but system design. Attention moves toward incentives, feedback loops, curriculum structure, and learning conditions.
Another formulation:

“Students do not understand why the material matters.”

This frame moves the problem toward meaning, relevance, and motivation.
Another:

“The course assumes background knowledge that students do not yet have.”

Now the issue becomes prerequisite structure.
Each sentence creates a different problem.
The facts may overlap, but the path of thought changes.
This is why framing is powerful: it does not merely describe a problem after it exists. It helps create the problem that will be reasoned about.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
section: 2.4 The Hidden Structure of a Problem Statement
text:
2.4 The Hidden Structure of a Problem Statement
A problem statement usually contains more structure than it appears to contain.
Even a short sentence may imply an entire architecture of thought.
Consider:

“The team is moving too slowly.”

This sentence contains several hidden assumptions:

there is a desirable speed
the current speed is below that standard
the team is the relevant unit of analysis
slowness is the key issue
speed is the dimension being evaluated
improvement means acceleration

But the same situation could be framed differently:

“The team lacks decision clarity.”

Now the issue is not speed itself, but decision structure.
Or:

“The project has too many unresolved dependencies.”

Now the issue is not the team’s performance, but project architecture.
Or:

“The organization rewards caution more than delivery.”

Now the issue becomes incentive design.
Each statement does more than describe. It configures the field of possible explanation and action.
A problem frame usually contains the following elements:








Element
Question
Function




Object
What is the issue about?
Defines the center of attention


Boundary
Where does the problem begin and end?
Prevents uncontrolled expansion


Condition
What is the current state?
Describes the situation


Standard
Compared to what is this a problem?
Establishes why it matters


Cause
What produces or sustains it?
Guides explanation


Agent
Who or what can act?
Defines responsibility and leverage


Consequence
What happens if it continues?
Establishes stakes


Direction
What kind of change is needed?
Opens the path toward solution



A strong problem frame makes these elements explicit.
A weak problem frame leaves them hidden.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.5 Symptoms, Problems, and Causes
One of the most common failures in deliberate thinking is confusing symptoms with problems.
A symptom is what appears.
A problem is the structured condition that requires attention.
A cause is what produces or maintains that condition.
For example:

“Sales are declining.”

This is a symptom.
It may point to different problems:

the product no longer fits customer needs
the sales channel is weakening
competitors have improved
pricing is misaligned
the brand has lost trust
the market itself is shrinking
the sales team lacks capability
customer acquisition costs have become too high

Each problem has different causes and different responses.
If a symptom is mistaken for the problem, action becomes shallow.
The organization may say:

“Sales are declining, so we need more marketing.”

But if the real issue is product-market mismatch, more marketing only accelerates exposure to a weak offer.
Or it may say:

“Sales are declining, so the sales team must work harder.”

But if the market has shifted, effort does not solve structural misalignment.
A symptom demands observation.
A problem demands framing.
A cause demands investigation.
Deliberate language must keep these layers separate.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.6 Problem Framing as Attention Design
To frame a problem is to design attention.
Every formulation tells the mind where to look.
A frame can direct attention toward:

individuals
systems
incentives
processes
resources
timing
constraints
values
communication
coordination
external conditions

For example:

“He failed.”

This directs attention toward the individual.

“The process failed.”

This directs attention toward workflow.

“The incentive structure produced the wrong behavior.”

This directs attention toward systemic forces.

“The environment made the correct behavior difficult.”

This directs attention toward context.
None of these frames is automatically correct. Each may reveal something or hide something.
The function of deliberate language is not to choose frames casually, but to test them.
A mature thinker asks:

What does this frame make visible?
What does it hide?
What does it assume?
What action does it encourage?
What alternatives does it exclude?

A frame is useful only when it helps thought contact the real structure of the situation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.7 The Boundary of the Problem
A problem without boundaries becomes unmanageable.
If the boundary is too narrow, the real issue is missed.
If the boundary is too wide, the problem becomes impossible to act on.
For example:

“I cannot learn English well.”

This frame is too broad.
It may include pronunciation, vocabulary, grammar, listening, reading, speaking, writing, confidence, learning method, input volume, feedback quality, or daily environment.
A better frame might be:

“My spoken English is weak because I cannot quickly organize simple explanations in real time.”

This is narrower and more actionable.
It defines:

the domain: spoken English
the specific function: real-time explanation
the difficulty: organizing simple explanations quickly
the likely direction: practice with structured expression

A bounded problem can be worked on.
But the boundary must not become artificially narrow.
If someone says:

“I need to memorize more words.”

This may be too narrow if the deeper problem is not vocabulary quantity, but the inability to organize meaning into scenes, events, states, and relations.
Good framing therefore requires boundary adjustment.
The problem must be large enough to include the real cause, but small enough to support action.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.8 The Standard Behind the Problem
Something becomes a problem only in relation to a standard.
Without a standard, there is only description.
Consider:

“The project took three months.”

Is this a problem?
It depends.
If the expected duration was one month, it may indicate delay.
If the expected duration was six months, it may indicate efficiency.
If the project was exploratory, three months may be normal.
If the project was urgent, it may be unacceptable.
A problem statement always implies a gap:
Current State ≠ Desired State
The desired state may be explicit or hidden.
For example:

“The response time is slow.”

Hidden standard:

“The response time should be faster than it currently is.”

But how fast?

under one second?
under three seconds?
fast enough for casual use?
fast enough for real-time interaction?
fast enough to compete with other products?

The standard matters because it determines the seriousness of the problem and the direction of solution.
A vague standard produces vague judgment.
Deliberate language requires the speaker to ask:

What is the expected state?
Who defines that expectation?
Is the standard realistic?
Is the standard measurable?
Is the standard appropriate for this context?

Without a clear standard, the problem cannot be evaluated.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
section: 2.9 The Difference Between Problem and Discomfort
text:
2.9 The Difference Between Problem and Discomfort
Not every discomfort is a problem in the deliberate sense.
Discomfort is felt.
A problem is formulated.
Discomfort may be the starting signal, but it is not yet an object of judgment.
For example:

“I feel stuck.”

This is emotionally real, but structurally incomplete.
It may become different problem frames:

“I do not know which goal to prioritize.”
“I have too many possible paths and no selection criteria.”
“I lack a concrete next action.”
“I am avoiding a decision because the consequences are uncertain.”
“My current environment does not support the identity I am trying to build.”

Each formulation converts discomfort into a workable structure.
This conversion is central to deliberate language.
Language takes a vague pressure and turns it into a defined object of thought.
Until this happens, the mind can only circle the feeling.
Once it happens, judgment can begin.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.10 Types of Problem Frames
Different problems require different forms of framing.
A problem may be framed as a gap, a conflict, a constraint, a risk, a failure, a trade-off, or an opportunity.
Each form creates a different reasoning path.









Frame Type
Basic Form
Example
Typical Direction




Gap
Current state differs from desired state
“Our output quality is below the required standard.”
Improve capability or process


Conflict
Two demands compete
“We need speed, but also reliability.”
Trade-off and prioritization


Constraint
A limit blocks action
“We cannot expand because capital is limited.”
Resource design or constraint removal


Risk
Future harm is possible
“If dependency X fails, the system may collapse.”
Prevention or contingency


Failure
Something did not work as expected
“The launch failed to retain users.”
Diagnosis and correction


Opportunity
A possible gain is visible
“There is an unmet need in this market.”
Exploration and strategy


Ambiguity
Meaning or direction is unclear
“Different teams define success differently.”
Clarification and alignment


Dependency
One element relies on another
“The plan depends on a partner we do not control.”
Dependency management



The same situation can often be framed in more than one way.
For example, a startup with limited money may say:

“We do not have enough funding.”

This is a constraint frame.
But it may also say:

“We need to find a path that produces learning before capital runs out.”

This is a strategic frame.
The second formulation does not deny the constraint, but it organizes thought around action and sequencing.
A good frame does not merely state difficulty. It creates a path for reasoning.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.11 How Frames Produce Action Paths
Every problem frame implies an action path.
This is one of the most important principles of this chapter.
A formulation is never passive. It quietly suggests what should be done next.
Consider these different frames:







Problem Frame
Implied Action




“The employee is underperforming.”
Correct, train, replace, or manage the employee


“The role is poorly defined.”
Redesign responsibilities and expectations


“The team lacks feedback loops.”
Build review, measurement, and correction mechanisms


“The workload exceeds available capacity.”
Reduce scope, add resources, or change priorities


“The system rewards the wrong behavior.”
Redesign incentives


“The goal is unclear.”
Clarify desired outcomes and criteria



This is why problem framing has practical consequences.
It determines not only how people think, but what they attempt to change.
A frame aimed at personality produces moral judgment.
A frame aimed at process produces operational redesign.
A frame aimed at incentives produces structural intervention.
A frame aimed at goals produces clarification.
A frame aimed at constraints produces negotiation or redesign.
When people disagree about solutions, they often disagree earlier than they realize.
They are not only choosing different actions.
They are operating inside different problem frames.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.12 Reframing
Reframing is the act of changing the structure of the problem.
It does not mean pretending the issue is positive.
It does not mean using softer language.
It does not mean escaping reality.
Reframing means altering the object, boundary, cause, standard, or action direction of a problem so that thought can contact the situation more accurately.
A useful reframe may move:

from symptom to cause
from blame to structure
from event to pattern
from individual to system
from impossibility to constraint
from vague concern to concrete gap
from isolated action to strategic sequence

For example:

“I am bad at writing.”

This is a self-labeling frame.
A reframe:

“I lack a stable process for turning scattered ideas into structured paragraphs.”

Now the problem becomes operational.
Another example:

“The market does not want our product.”

This may be too final.
A reframe:

“We have not yet identified which user segment has a strong enough pain to adopt this product.”

Now inquiry becomes possible.
Another:

“The plan failed.”

A reframe:

“The plan depended on an assumption that turned out to be false.”

Now revision becomes possible.
Reframing is not cosmetic optimism.
It is structural correction.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.13 Bad Frames and Their Costs
A bad problem frame can cause damage even when everyone involved is intelligent.
Several failure modes are especially common.

2.13.1 The Label Frame
This frame turns a complex situation into a fixed label.
Examples:

“He is lazy.”
“The team is incompetent.”
“The product is bad.”
“The market is impossible.”

Labels stop inquiry.
They may contain partial truth, but they usually compress too much. Once a label is accepted, people stop asking what structure produced the observed behavior.


2.13.2 The Symptom Frame
This frame treats the visible signal as the problem itself.
Examples:

“Traffic is down.”
“Revenue is down.”
“Users are leaving.”
“Students are quiet.”

Symptoms matter, but they are not enough.
A symptom frame creates reactive action before diagnosis is complete.


2.13.3 The Blame Frame
This frame organizes the issue around fault.
Examples:

“Who caused this?”
“Which person failed?”
“Which team is responsible?”

Responsibility matters, especially in real systems. But when blame comes too early, it can block structural understanding.
The question should not only be:

“Who did this?”

but also:

“What conditions made this likely?”



2.13.4 The False Choice Frame
This frame presents a situation as if only two options exist.
Examples:

“Should we choose quality or speed?”
“Should I stay or leave?”
“Should we build or buy?”
“Should we centralize or decentralize?”

Sometimes binary choice is necessary. But often the binary hides a wider decision space.
The better question may be:

“Under what conditions can we preserve enough quality while increasing speed?”

or:

“Which parts should be centralized, and which parts should remain local?”



2.13.5 The Totalizing Frame
This frame expands the problem until it becomes too large to handle.
Examples:

“My life is not working.”
“The company is broken.”
“Education is failing.”
“Society has lost direction.”

These statements may express real concern, but they do not yet support deliberate action.
A totalizing frame must be decomposed.


2.13.6 The Solution-First Frame
This frame smuggles a preferred solution into the problem statement.
Examples:

“How do we get everyone back to the office?”
“How do we make users use this feature?”
“How do I force myself to study more?”

These questions assume the solution before the problem has been examined.
A better formulation asks:

“What work requires physical presence?”
“What user need is this feature supposed to serve?”
“What prevents consistent study from happening?”

Good framing delays solution long enough to understand the problem.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.14 The Grammar of Problem Framing
Problem framing can be treated as a grammar.
A basic problem frame can be written as:
In [context], [subject] experiences [undesired condition]
because / while / under [cause, constraint, or uncertainty],
which affects [stakes],
so the decision is [what must be clarified, changed, or chosen].
For example:
In the current learning environment, students struggle to explain ideas in English
because they lack reusable structures for organizing meaning,
which affects speaking fluency and confidence,
so the decision is how to design practice around structured explanation rather than isolated word memorization.
This frame is stronger than:
Students need better English.
Because it defines:

context
subject
condition
cause
stakes
decision direction

Another example:
In the product launch process, the team repeatedly misses deadlines
because major dependencies are discovered too late,
which creates rework and damages trust,
so the decision is how to surface dependencies earlier and sequence work more realistically.
This is stronger than:
The team is slow.
The grammar does not need to be used mechanically every time. But it reveals what a complete problem frame usually contains.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
section: 2.15 A Practical Method for Framing Problems
text:
2.15 A Practical Method for Framing Problems
A useful problem-framing process can move through seven questions.

2.15.1 What is happening?
Begin with observation.
Do not interpret too quickly.
Ask:

What is occurring?
What signals are visible?
What changed?
What is repeated?
What is abnormal?

At this stage, the goal is not explanation. The goal is contact with reality.


2.15.2 Why is this considered a problem?
Identify the standard.
Ask:

What expectation is being violated?
What goal is being blocked?
What value is being threatened?
Who considers this a problem, and why?

This prevents vague dissatisfaction from pretending to be analysis.


2.15.3 What is the object of the problem?
Define the central object.
Ask:

Is this about a person?
A process?
A system?
A relationship?
A resource?
A constraint?
A decision?
A mismatch?

Changing the object changes the problem.


2.15.4 Where is the boundary?
Define scope.
Ask:

What is included?
What is excluded?
What level are we working at?
Is this local or systemic?
Is this short-term or long-term?

A frame without boundary cannot guide action.


2.15.5 What might be causing or sustaining it?
Move from symptom to structure.
Ask:

What conditions produce this?
What incentives maintain it?
What dependencies shape it?
What assumptions are involved?
What feedback loops exist?

Causality should be treated carefully. Early frames should allow revision.


2.15.6 What action paths does this frame imply?
Make the hidden direction visible.
Ask:

If we accept this frame, what will we try to do?
Who will be asked to change?
What resources will be used?
What alternatives will be ignored?
What risks does this frame create?

A frame should be judged partly by the quality of action it opens.


2.15.7 What alternative frames are possible?
Generate competing formulations.
Ask:

Can this be framed as a gap?
As a constraint?
As a trade-off?
As a coordination failure?
As a learning problem?
As a system design problem?
As an uncertainty problem?

The first frame is rarely the best frame.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
section: 2.16 Example: From Vague Problem to Deliberate Frame
text:
2.16 Example: From Vague Problem to Deliberate Frame
Consider the statement:

“I cannot make progress.”

This is too vague for deliberate thinking.
It may be developed through several frames.

2.16.1 Frame 1: Motivation Problem

“I cannot make progress because I lack motivation.”

This directs action toward discipline, emotion, and willpower.
Possible actions:

create routines
increase accountability
use rewards
reduce friction



2.16.2 Frame 2: Goal Problem

“I cannot make progress because my goal is not clearly defined.”

This directs action toward clarification.
Possible actions:

define the desired outcome
set success criteria
distinguish primary and secondary goals



2.16.3 Frame 3: Decision Space Problem

“I cannot make progress because I see too many possible paths and lack criteria for choosing.”

This directs action toward option structuring.
Possible actions:

list alternatives
define criteria
compare trade-offs
choose a path for a fixed period



2.16.4 Frame 4: System Problem

“I cannot make progress because my environment continuously breaks concentration.”

This directs action toward context design.
Possible actions:

change workspace
block distractions
redesign schedule
reduce interruptions



2.16.5 Frame 5: Capability Problem

“I cannot make progress because the task requires skills I have not yet built.”

This directs action toward learning sequence.
Possible actions:

identify missing subskills
build practice loops
reduce task difficulty
get feedback

Each frame may be true in part. The task is not to choose one instantly, but to test which frame best explains the situation and opens useful action.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.17 Framing and Responsibility
Problem framing also distributes responsibility.
This must be handled carefully.
A frame can place responsibility on:

the individual
the team
the system
leadership
environment
constraints
historical conditions
external forces

For example:

“The worker made a mistake.”

This assigns responsibility to the worker.

“The process allowed a single mistake to cause system failure.”

This assigns responsibility to process design.

“The organization did not create adequate review mechanisms.”

This assigns responsibility to governance.
The point is not to remove individual responsibility. The point is to locate responsibility accurately.
A mature frame can hold multiple layers at once:
The individual made an error,
but the system lacked safeguards,
and the organization had normalized a risky workflow.
This kind of frame supports better action because it avoids both extremes:

blaming only the individual
dissolving responsibility entirely into the system

Deliberate language must preserve responsibility while expanding understanding.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.18 Framing and Time Horizon
Problems also change depending on time horizon.
A short-term frame asks:

“What must be fixed now?”

A medium-term frame asks:

“What pattern must be changed?”

A long-term frame asks:

“What structure must be redesigned?”

For example:

“We missed the deadline.”

Short-term frame:

“How do we recover this delivery?”

Medium-term frame:

“Why do we repeatedly underestimate project complexity?”

Long-term frame:

“How should our planning system change so that scope, dependency, and uncertainty are represented earlier?”

Each horizon has a different function.
Short-term framing supports response.
Medium-term framing supports correction.
Long-term framing supports redesign.
Bad deliberation often occurs when these horizons are confused.
A person may use long-term philosophical analysis when immediate action is required.
Or they may use short-term fixes when structural redesign is needed.
A good problem frame names the time horizon of concern.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.19 Framing and Level of Analysis
A problem may exist at different levels.
For example, in a software product, a failure may be framed at the level of:

interface
feature
user workflow
product positioning
technical architecture
team process
business model
market timing

A surface-level frame might say:

“The button is confusing.”

A deeper frame may say:

“The workflow does not match the user’s mental model.”

An even deeper frame may say:

“The product assumes a user behavior that does not exist.”

Each level changes the action.
The art of framing is not always to go deeper. Sometimes the immediate level is enough. But deliberate thinking must know which level it is operating on.
The central question is:

At what level must the problem be framed in order for action to be effective?

If the frame is too shallow, action treats symptoms.
If the frame is too deep, action becomes abstract and slow.
A good frame has the right level for the decision at hand.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
section: 2.20 The Problem Frame as a Decision Gate
text:
2.20 The Problem Frame as a Decision Gate
Problem framing is the gate through which the rest of deliberation passes.
Once a problem is framed, the next steps become possible:

goals can be clarified
criteria can be defined
options can be generated
alternatives can be compared
risks can be assessed
judgments can be made
plans can be formed

But if the frame is unstable, everything downstream becomes unstable.
A bad frame contaminates the entire decision process.
If the wrong problem is defined, the right solution may still fail.
This is why problem framing must be treated as a first-class act of deliberate language.
It is not a preliminary paragraph.
It is not a background section.
It is not merely context.
It is the construction of the object that judgment will operate on.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.21 Problem Framing Template
A deliberate problem frame can be built using the following template:
The issue is not simply [surface symptom].
The deeper problem is [structured problem],
in the context of [situation],
where [subject/system] is trying to [goal],
but faces [constraint/conflict/uncertainty],
leading to [consequence/stakes].
Therefore, the central question is:
how to [decision/action direction] under [conditions].
Example:
The issue is not simply that users are inactive.
The deeper problem is that the product does not create a clear first success moment,
in the context of a crowded market where users abandon tools quickly,
where the product is trying to become part of a repeated workflow,
but faces unclear onboarding, weak feedback, and low perceived value,
leading to poor retention.
Therefore, the central question is:
how to redesign the early user experience so that users reach a meaningful result before attention is lost.
This template separates symptom, structure, context, goal, constraint, consequence, and decision direction.
It turns a vague concern into a deliberative object.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.22 Minimal Form
In practice, a shorter version is often enough:
The problem is [structured condition],
not merely [surface symptom],
because [reason or sustaining structure],
which matters because [stakes].
Example:
The problem is a lack of reusable explanation structures,
not merely weak vocabulary,
because the learner cannot organize meaning quickly during speech,
which matters because real-time expression requires structure before fluency.
This minimal form is especially useful when beginning analysis.
It prevents premature solution and forces the problem to become explicit.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 2  Framing the Problem
text:
2.23 Chapter Summary
This chapter has argued that problem framing is the first act of deliberate language.
A problem is not simply given by reality. It is constructed through language by defining an object, boundary, standard, cause, agent, consequence, and direction.
Different formulations produce different paths of thought and action.
A weak frame may confuse symptoms with causes, compress complexity into labels, assign blame too early, assume false choices, or smuggle solutions into the problem statement.
A strong frame makes the issue thinkable, bounded, testable, and actionable.
The purpose of framing is not to make a problem sound better.
It is to make the problem structurally available to judgment.
Once the problem has been framed, deliberation can move to the next task:
clarifying goals, criteria, and stakes.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.1 Language Happens Somewhere
Speech is never isolated.
A sentence does not appear in empty space.
It is spoken by someone, to someone, at a particular time, in a particular setting, within a particular relationship, under particular stakes.
This is why the same sentence can mean different things in different situations.
Consider the sentence:
Its grammatical structure is simple.
Its pragmatic meaning is not.
Said by a friend after a misunderstanding, it may signal repair.
Said by a manager after a mistake, it may signal evaluation.
Said by a partner late at night, it may signal emotional seriousness.
Said by a teacher to a student, it may signal correction.
Said by a client to a contractor, it may signal dissatisfaction.
The words remain the same.
The situation changes the meaning.
This chapter studies the minimal structure of a speech event. It asks:
The answer is not only grammar, vocabulary, or sentence structure.
A speech event requires at least six elements:
These elements form the basic situation of speech.
Without them, language cannot be fully understood.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
“We need to talk.”







What must be present for language to function as situated action?



Speaker
Listener
Timing
Setting
Relationship
Stakes
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.2 Speech as Situated Action
In earlier layers of language, the primary question may be:

What does this sentence mean?

In pragmatic language, the question changes:

What is this speech doing in this situation?

A sentence may describe, request, warn, promise, refuse, comfort, pressure, accuse, invite, negotiate, clarify, command, or repair.
Its function depends on the situation in which it appears.
For example:

“It is cold here.”

As a description, it means the temperature is low.
But in real interaction, it may function as:

a complaint
a request to close the window
an indirect criticism
a hint
a shared observation
a test of whether the listener is attentive

The literal content is only one layer.
The pragmatic function depends on who says it, to whom, when, where, under what relationship, and with what consequence.
Language is therefore not merely a system of sentences.
It is a system of situated moves.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
section: 3.3 The Minimal Structure of a Speech Event
text:
3.3 The Minimal Structure of a Speech Event
A speech event can be understood as a structured interaction.
At minimum, it contains the following elements:








Element
Core Question
Function




Speaker
Who is speaking?
Establishes source, intention, authority, responsibility


Listener
Who is being addressed?
Establishes audience, interpretation, obligation


Timing
When is it said?
Shapes urgency, appropriateness, and meaning


Setting
Where and under what conditions is it said?
Defines context and available norms


Relationship
What is the relation between speaker and listener?
Shapes power, distance, intimacy, permission


Stakes
What matters if this speech succeeds or fails?
Defines consequence and pressure



These elements are often invisible because competent speakers use them automatically.
But when they are ignored, communication breaks down.
A person may say the right words to the wrong listener.
Or say the right thing at the wrong time.
Or speak with the wrong degree of formality.
Or treat a high-stakes situation as casual.
Or use direct language in a relationship that requires tact.
Or use indirect language when clarity is required.
Pragmatic competence begins when language is understood inside its situation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.4 Speaker
Every speech event begins with a speaker.
But “speaker” does not only mean the person producing words.
The speaker is the source of the speech act.
The speaker brings:

intention
position
knowledge
authority
emotion
responsibility
identity
credibility

The same sentence changes depending on who says it.
Consider:

“You should revise this.”

Said by a teacher, it may be instruction.
Said by a peer, it may be advice.
Said by a client, it may be a requirement.
Said by a stranger, it may feel intrusive.
Said by an expert, it may carry authority.
Said by someone careless, it may be ignored.
The speaker’s position affects how the words are received.
A speaker must therefore ask:

What role am I speaking from?
Do I have the authority to say this?
Am I offering, requesting, instructing, judging, or warning?
What responsibility do I carry after saying this?
How will my identity affect interpretation?

Speech is never detached from the speaker’s position.
The speaker is part of the meaning.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.5 Listener
Speech is addressed.
Even when someone appears to speak generally, the speech assumes an audience.
The listener is not a passive receiver.
The listener actively interprets, evaluates, accepts, resists, misunderstands, or responds.
A sentence that is clear to one listener may be unclear to another.
For example:

“Please handle this soon.”

To a close colleague who knows the project, this may be enough.
To a new employee, it may be too vague.
To a contractor, it may require a deadline.
To a child, it may require concrete steps.
To a legal or operational team, it may require written specification.
The listener determines what must be made explicit.
A speaker must therefore ask:

What does the listener already know?
What does the listener not know?
What does the listener care about?
What authority does the listener have?
What action can the listener take?
What misunderstanding is likely?
What emotional state might the listener be in?

Good pragmatic language is listener-aware.
It does not merely express what the speaker wants to say.
It shapes speech according to what the listener can understand, accept, and act on.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.6 Timing
Timing changes meaning.
The same words said before an event, during an event, and after an event do not have the same force.
Consider:

“Be careful.”

Before the action, it is a warning.
During the action, it may be urgent guidance.
After the accident, it may sound like blame.
Timing affects:

urgency
appropriateness
emotional impact
perceived intention
available action
responsibility

Some speech acts must happen early.
A warning given too late becomes accusation.
A clarification given too late becomes excuse.
An apology given too late becomes damage control.
A decision given too late becomes abandonment.
A correction given too early may feel premature.
Timing is not external to language.
It is part of the speech act.
For example:

“I disagree.”

Said during planning, it may improve the decision.
Said after execution has already begun, it may disrupt coordination.
Said after failure, it may sound like self-protection.
A competent speaker asks:

Is this the right moment?
Is action still possible?
Is the listener ready to hear this?
Should this be said before, during, or after the event?
Will delay change the meaning?
Will early speech prevent later harm?

Timing gives language its practical force.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.7 Setting
Speech occurs in a setting.
The setting includes physical place, institutional context, medium, social norms, and surrounding conditions.
A sentence spoken in private is not the same as a sentence spoken in public.
A sentence spoken in a meeting is not the same as a sentence written in a report.
A sentence sent in a casual chat is not the same as a sentence sent in a legal document.
Setting determines what kind of language is appropriate.
For example:

“This plan is weak.”

In a private review session, this may be direct feedback.
In a public meeting, it may be humiliation.
In a written evaluation, it may require evidence.
In a brainstorming session, it may suppress creativity.
In a crisis room, it may be necessary.
The setting changes the norms.
A speaker must ask:

Is this public or private?
Is this formal or informal?
Is this synchronous or asynchronous?
Is this written or spoken?
Is this casual, professional, legal, emotional, or operational?
What norms govern this setting?
What can be safely said here?
What should be moved to another setting?

A speech event does not only depend on people.
It depends on the environment in which speech appears.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.8 Relationship
Speech is shaped by relationship.
Language between strangers differs from language between close friends.
Language between peers differs from language between manager and employee.
Language between parent and child differs from language between client and vendor.
Relationship affects:

directness
politeness
permission
authority
emotional tone
risk of offense
degree of explanation required
acceptable forms of disagreement

Consider:

“You are wrong.”

Between close collaborators with strong trust, this may be acceptable.
Between strangers, it may be aggressive.
From a manager to an employee, it may feel disciplinary.
From a junior employee to a senior leader, it may require careful framing.
In an academic debate, it may be normal if supported by evidence.
The relationship determines how language should be calibrated.
A speaker must ask:

What is my relationship to the listener?
Is there trust?
Is there power difference?
Is directness permitted?
Is the relationship fragile or stable?
Does this speech strengthen or threaten the relationship?
Do I need to preserve dignity while delivering content?

Pragmatic language is not only about saying what is true.
It is about saying what is true in a way that the relationship can carry.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.9 Stakes
Every speech event has stakes.
Sometimes the stakes are low:

“Where should we eat?”

Sometimes the stakes are high:

“This treatment may not work.”
“We need to cut the project.”
“Your performance is below expectation.”
“I cannot agree to this contract.”
“The system may fail if we continue.”

Stakes determine how carefully language must be designed.
High-stakes speech usually requires:

precision
evidence
emotional control
explicit responsibility
clear next steps
reduced ambiguity
awareness of consequences

Low-stakes speech can tolerate more looseness.
A speaker must ask:

What happens if I say this badly?
What happens if I do not say this?
Who may be affected?
Is this reversible or irreversible?
Does this create obligation?
Does this change trust, money, safety, status, or future action?

Stakes transform language from casual expression into responsible action.
The higher the stakes, the less language can rely on implication alone.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.10 The Six-Part Situation Model
The situation of speech can be represented as a minimal model:
Speaker → Listener
   |          |
Timing — Setting
   |          |
Relationship — Stakes
This model is not a rigid formula.
It is a diagnostic tool.
When communication fails, one of these elements is often misread.
The speaker may misjudge the listener.
The listener may misread the speaker’s intention.
The timing may be poor.
The setting may be inappropriate.
The relationship may not support the level of directness.
The stakes may be higher than the speaker realizes.
A speech event is successful when these elements are aligned.
It fails when one or more of them are mismatched.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.11 Example: Same Sentence, Different Situations
Consider the sentence:

“Can you finish this today?”

Its meaning depends on the situation.

3.11.1 Manager to Employee
The sentence may function as a task assignment.
The employee may hear:

“This is expected.”

The relationship contains authority.
The stakes may involve performance.


3.11.2 Peer to Peer
The sentence may function as coordination.
The listener may hear:

“Can I rely on you?”

The relationship is more horizontal.
The stakes involve mutual dependency.


3.11.3 Client to Contractor
The sentence may function as pressure.
The listener may hear:

“Delivery is being demanded.”

The setting may imply contractual expectation.


3.11.4 Friend to Friend
The sentence may function as a favor request.
The listener may hear:

“I need help.”

The relationship may soften the pressure.


3.11.5 During a Crisis
The sentence may function as urgent command.
The listener may hear:

“This must happen immediately.”

Timing and stakes raise the force of the sentence.
The sentence is grammatically identical in all cases.
But pragmatically, it is different each time.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.12 When the Situation Is Misread
Many communication failures are not failures of vocabulary or grammar.
They are failures of situation reading.

3.12.1 Wrong Listener
The speaker addresses someone who cannot act.
Example:

Complaining to a peer about a decision only leadership can change.

The speech may express frustration, but it does not reach the responsible agent.


3.12.2 Wrong Timing
The speaker says something too early or too late.
Example:

Giving detailed criticism during the first fragile stage of idea generation.

The content may be valid, but the timing damages the process.


3.12.3 Wrong Setting
The speaker uses a public setting for speech that requires privacy.
Example:

Correcting someone harshly in front of a group.

The issue may be real, but the setting adds humiliation.


3.12.4 Wrong Relationship Calibration
The speaker uses a tone that the relationship cannot support.
Example:

Speaking with extreme directness to someone with whom trust has not been built.

The message may be accurate, but the relationship cannot carry it.


3.12.5 Wrong Stakes Assessment
The speaker treats a high-stakes issue casually.
Example:

Giving vague instructions for a task that affects safety, money, or legal responsibility.

The language is too loose for the consequence.
These failures show that effective speech requires situational intelligence.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.13 Speech Situation and Responsibility
The more consequential the speech, the more responsibility the speaker carries for framing the situation correctly.
This does not mean the speaker controls all interpretation.
Listeners also carry responsibility.
They must listen carefully, ask clarifying questions, and avoid careless interpretation.
But the speaker has first responsibility for designing the speech act.
Before speaking, one should ask:
Who am I speaking as?
Who am I speaking to?
Why now?
Why here?
What is our relationship?
What is at stake?
These questions convert speech from impulse into deliberate action.
They are especially important in:

feedback
apology
negotiation
disagreement
instruction
refusal
warning
decision communication
conflict repair

In such cases, language does not merely transmit content.
It changes the social situation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.14 Direct and Indirect Speech
The situation also shapes whether speech should be direct or indirect.
Direct speech is useful when clarity, urgency, or accountability is required.
Example:

“Please submit the report by 5 p.m. today.”

Indirect speech is useful when politeness, relationship preservation, or emotional sensitivity matters.
Example:

“It would be helpful if we could have the report before the end of the day.”

Neither directness nor indirectness is inherently better.
The correct form depends on the situation.
High urgency often requires directness.
Fragile relationships may require tact.
High-stakes decisions require explicitness.
Emotional repair may require softness.
Operational execution requires clarity.
A pragmatic speaker does not ask only:

“Is this sentence correct?”

The speaker asks:

“Is this sentence appropriate for this situation?”
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
section: 3.15 The Situation Determines the Force of Speech
text:
3.15 The Situation Determines the Force of Speech
Speech has force.
It can request, command, invite, warn, refuse, promise, apologize, evaluate, approve, reject, or authorize.
But the force is not always visible from the words alone.
Consider:

“You may leave now.”

From a friend, it may sound strange.
From a teacher, it may grant permission.
From a judge, it may carry institutional authority.
From a security officer, it may be an instruction.
From a host, it may be rude depending on tone and setting.
The force of speech depends on the social situation.
This is why pragmatic language cannot be reduced to sentence meaning.
The same sentence may carry different force because the speaker occupies a different role, the listener has a different obligation, or the setting gives the words institutional weight.
To understand speech, one must understand its force.
To design speech, one must design its force intentionally.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.16 Situation-Aware Speech Design
A practical speech design process can begin with six questions.

3.16.1 Who is speaking?
Define role and authority.
Am I speaking as a friend, teacher, manager, parent, peer, expert, client, citizen, or learner?


3.16.2 Who is listening?
Define audience and capacity.
What does the listener know, need, expect, fear, or misunderstand?


3.16.3 Why now?
Define timing.
Is this the right moment for warning, correction, request, decision, or repair?


3.16.4 Where is this happening?
Define setting.
Should this be said in public, private, writing, meeting, chat, document, or formal channel?


3.16.5 What is the relationship?
Define social calibration.
How much directness, explanation, politeness, or emotional care does this relationship require?


3.16.6 What is at stake?
Define consequence.
What happens if this speech succeeds, fails, or is misunderstood?
These questions form the basic discipline of pragmatic speech.
They prevent language from being treated as disembodied text.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.17 Example: Giving Feedback
Suppose a manager needs to give feedback to an employee.
A situation-blind version might be:

“Your work is not good enough.”

This sentence may be clear, but it is pragmatically weak.
It lacks specificity, direction, and relationship care.
A situation-aware version would consider the six elements.

3.17.1 Speaker
The speaker is a manager.
The speech carries authority and may affect confidence, evaluation, and future behavior.


3.17.2 Listener
The listener may need concrete guidance, not merely judgment.


3.17.3 Timing
Feedback should be close enough to the event to be useful, but not delivered in a moment of public embarrassment.


3.17.4 Setting
Private setting is usually better than public correction.


3.17.5 Relationship
The tone should preserve respect while still being honest.


3.17.6 Stakes
The employee’s performance, trust, and development are affected.
A stronger formulation:

“The current draft does not yet meet the standard we need for client delivery. The main issue is not effort, but structure: the argument is difficult to follow, and the evidence is not connected clearly to the recommendation. Please revise the draft by reorganizing it around three claims, adding supporting evidence under each one, and sending me the revised version by tomorrow afternoon.”

This speech is more effective because it defines:

the issue
the standard
the reason
the direction
the next action
the deadline

It is not merely more polite.
It is more structurally responsible.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.18 Example: Making a Request
Consider a request:

“Help me with this.”

This may be acceptable in a close relationship with shared context.
But in many situations, it is too vague.
A situation-aware request defines:

what help is needed
why the listener is being asked
when it is needed
what level of effort is expected
what outcome is desired

For example:

“Could you review the introduction section today and tell me whether the problem statement is clear? I do not need line editing yet; I only need feedback on structure.”

This version reduces misunderstanding.
It respects the listener’s time.
It defines the task.
It names the boundary.
It clarifies the expected response.
Good requests are not merely polite.
They are designed to make cooperation possible.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.19 Example: Disagreement
Disagreement is one of the clearest tests of pragmatic competence.
A situation-blind disagreement says:

“That is wrong.”

Sometimes this is acceptable. Often it is not enough.
A situation-aware disagreement may say:

“I see the direction, but I think one assumption needs to be tested. We are assuming users will change their workflow immediately, and I am not sure we have evidence for that.”

This formulation does several things:

preserves connection
identifies the specific disagreement
avoids attacking the person
moves the discussion toward evidence
keeps the decision process open

In high-trust technical debate, more direct language may be acceptable:

“I disagree with the architecture choice because it creates a single point of failure.”

Here, directness is useful because the issue is concrete and the stakes may be high.
The question is not whether disagreement should be direct or indirect.
The question is:

What form of disagreement fits this situation?
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.20 Speech Event Template
A speech event can be analyzed using the following template:
In [setting], [speaker] says [utterance] to [listener]
at [timing],
within [relationship],
under [stakes],
in order to [speech function].
Example:
In a private review meeting, a manager tells a junior employee that the report needs revision
after the first draft is submitted,
within a developmental work relationship,
under the stakes of client delivery and employee growth,
in order to correct the work while preserving confidence and direction.
This template makes the speech situation visible.
It helps distinguish sentence meaning from pragmatic function.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.21 Minimal Diagnostic Checklist
Before important speech, use the following checklist:
Speaker: Who am I speaking as?
Listener: Who needs to hear this?
Timing: Why now?
Setting: Is this the right place or medium?
Relationship: What tone can this relationship carry?
Stakes: What happens if this is misunderstood?
Function: What am I trying to do with this speech?
This checklist is especially useful before:

giving criticism
making requests
setting boundaries
apologizing
refusing
negotiating
warning
making promises
communicating decisions

In ordinary low-stakes speech, such analysis happens quickly and implicitly.
In high-stakes speech, it should become explicit.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 3  The Situation of Speech
text:
3.22 Chapter Summary
Speech is not only made of words.
It is made of words situated within a human event.
The minimal structure of a speech event includes:
Speaker
Listener
Timing
Setting
Relationship
Stakes
These elements shape what speech means, what force it carries, and what consequences it produces.
The same sentence may function differently depending on who says it, who hears it, when it is said, where it is said, what relationship exists, and what is at stake.
Pragmatic language begins when speech is no longer treated as isolated expression, but as situated action.
To speak well is not only to form correct sentences.
It is to understand the situation in which those sentences must act.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.1 The Sentence Is Not the Whole Meaning
A sentence has form.
It has words, grammar, structure, and literal content.
But in real communication, meaning is rarely contained inside the sentence alone.
A sentence is only one part of a larger event.
It is spoken by someone, to someone, at a particular moment, within a specific situation, against a background of shared knowledge, expectations, assumptions, and purposes.
This is why actual meaning often exceeds sentence form.
Consider the sentence:
As a sentence, it describes time.
But in real situations, it may mean:
The words do not explicitly say any of these.
Yet listeners often understand them.
This is because human communication depends not only on what is said, but also on what is implied, assumed, inferred, and understood from context.
The sentence provides material.
The situation completes the meaning.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
“It is getting late.”




“We should leave.”
“Please finish quickly.”
“I am tired.”
“You have stayed too long.”
“We are running out of time.”
“This conversation should end.”
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.2 Sentence Meaning and Utterance Meaning
A basic distinction is necessary.
Sentence meaning is the meaning supported by the linguistic form itself.
Utterance meaning is what the sentence means when used by a speaker in a concrete situation.
The same sentence can have one sentence meaning but many utterance meanings.
For example:

“Can you open the window?”

Sentence meaning:

The speaker asks whether the listener has the ability to open the window.

Utterance meaning:

The speaker is requesting that the listener open the window.

The listener does not usually answer:

“Yes, I can.”

The listener understands the intended action.
This shows that actual meaning is not produced by grammar alone.
It requires inference.
The listener must understand that the speaker is not merely asking about ability. The speaker is using a question form to make a request.
This difference is central to pragmatic language.
A sentence is a structure of expression.
An utterance is a move in a situation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.3 The Layers of Meaning
Actual meaning can be understood as layered.
A sentence may carry multiple kinds of meaning at the same time.








Layer
Question
Example




Literal Meaning
What do the words say?
“The window is open.”


Contextual Meaning
What does it mean here?
The room is cold because the window is open.


Implied Meaning
What is being suggested without being stated?
Please close the window.


Relational Meaning
What does this do to the relationship?
The speaker expects the listener to notice and act.


Action Meaning
What response does it invite?
Close the window, explain, ignore, or negotiate.


Emotional Meaning
What feeling does it carry?
Irritation, discomfort, politeness, urgency.



A listener who understands only the literal meaning may miss the speech act.
A listener who understands the implied meaning but ignores the relational meaning may respond correctly but damage the relationship.
A listener who understands the action meaning but misses the emotional meaning may solve the task while failing the human situation.
Meaning is therefore not flat.
It is layered, situated, and inferential.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.4 Context as Meaning Infrastructure
Context is not decoration around language.
Context is part of the meaning-making system.
A sentence becomes meaningful through the conditions that surround it.
Context may include:

physical environment
prior conversation
shared history
social relationship
cultural norms
institutional roles
current goals
emotional atmosphere
expected next actions
what has already been said
what does not need to be said

For example:

“That was interesting.”

This sentence can mean many things.
After a strong academic presentation, it may express genuine appreciation.
After a strange proposal in a meeting, it may be polite hesitation.
After a failed performance, it may soften criticism.
After a shocking story, it may express surprise.
After a boring event, it may be ironic.
The sentence is the same.
The context changes its force.
A competent listener does not process the sentence alone.
The listener combines sentence form with contextual evidence.
This is why language cannot be fully understood as isolated text.
Meaning lives in context.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.5 Shared Background
Much of communication depends on shared background.
Shared background includes what speaker and listener both know, assume, remember, or treat as obvious.
Without shared background, sentences must become longer and more explicit.
With strong shared background, speech can become compressed.
For example, two close collaborators may say:

“Same issue as last time.”

This may be enough.
They both know:

which issue
when it happened
what caused it
why it matters
what usually follows
what should be done next

To an outsider, the sentence is almost meaningless.
To insiders, it may carry a full structure.
Shared background allows language to be economical.
But it also creates risk.
If the speaker assumes shared background that the listener does not actually have, misunderstanding occurs.
For example:

“Just use the standard process.”

This works only if the listener knows what “standard process” means.
If the listener does not know, the sentence becomes empty authority.
The speaker may think they were clear.
The listener may pretend to understand.
The task may fail.
This is one of the most common problems in real communication:

The speaker compresses meaning based on assumed background, but the listener does not possess the background needed to decompress it.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.6 Inference
Actual meaning requires inference.
Inference is the process by which the listener moves from what is said to what is meant.
For example:

“I have an early meeting tomorrow.”

In many contexts, this may mean:

“I want to leave now.”

The listener infers this because the sentence appears at a certain moment, perhaps late at night, during a social gathering, when departure is socially delicate.
The speaker does not say:

“I want to leave.”

Instead, the speaker gives a reason that allows the listener to understand the intended action.
This kind of meaning depends on inference.
The listener asks, often unconsciously:

Why did the speaker say this now?
What is relevant about this sentence?
What action does this make available?
What is the speaker avoiding saying directly?
What background knowledge makes this sentence meaningful?

Inference turns sentence content into communicative meaning.
Without inference, conversation becomes mechanical.
With inference, language becomes efficient, subtle, and socially adaptive.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
section: 4.7 What Is Said and What Is Meant
text:
4.7 What Is Said and What Is Meant
A core feature of pragmatic language is the gap between what is said and what is meant.
This gap is not necessarily deception.
It is often normal communication.
People do not always say everything directly because direct speech may be unnecessary, inefficient, impolite, too forceful, or socially inappropriate.
For example:

“Do you know what time it is?”

In many contexts, this does not merely ask whether the listener knows the time.
It may mean:

“Please tell me the time.”
“You are late.”
“We need to hurry.”
“It is time to leave.”

The actual meaning depends on situation.
Human beings routinely communicate through this gap.
The important question is not only:

What did the speaker say?

but:

What was the speaker doing by saying this?
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.8 Why Meaning Exceeds Sentence Form
Meaning exceeds sentence form for several reasons.

4.8.1 The Sentence Is Incomplete Without Reference
Many sentences contain words whose meaning depends on the situation.
Examples:

“Put it there.”
“She already knows.”
“That one is better.”
“We should do it tomorrow.”
“This is not acceptable.”

Words like “it,” “there,” “she,” “that,” “we,” “tomorrow,” and “this” require context.
Without context, the sentence cannot be fully interpreted.


4.8.2 The Sentence Does Not Contain the Speaker’s Purpose
A sentence may have literal content, but purpose must be inferred.
For example:

“The door is open.”

Possible purposes:

describing a fact
asking someone to close it
warning about security
complaining about cold air
suggesting someone entered
pointing out carelessness

The sentence alone does not determine purpose.


4.8.3 The Sentence Does Not Contain Social Meaning
The same words may be respectful, rude, intimate, distant, friendly, threatening, or ironic depending on relationship and tone.
For example:

“Nice job.”

This may be genuine praise.
It may also be sarcasm.
Sentence form alone is insufficient.


4.8.4 The Sentence Does Not Contain All Assumptions
People often leave assumptions unstated.
For example:

“We cannot launch yet.”

Hidden assumptions may include:

the product has quality requirements
launch creates public exposure
errors would damage trust
the current version has unresolved risks
waiting is less costly than failure

The sentence compresses a reasoning structure.


4.8.5 The Sentence Does Not Determine the Required Response
A sentence may invite different responses.
For example:

“I am worried about this plan.”

The speaker may want:

reassurance
revision
discussion
evidence
delay
support
a decision

The listener must infer the expected response.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.9 The Role of Relevance
Listeners assume that speech is relevant.
When someone says something, the listener usually assumes there is a reason.
This assumption drives inference.
For example, imagine two people standing near an open window.
One says:

“It is cold.”

The listener does not treat this as random meteorological information.
The listener asks:

Why is this being said here and now?

Because the window is open, the sentence becomes relevant as an indirect request.
If the same sentence is spoken during a weather report, it has a different function.
Relevance connects language to situation.
A sentence becomes meaningful because the listener searches for why it matters now.
This is one reason communication can be efficient.
Speakers do not need to state every connection explicitly.
Listeners use relevance to reconstruct the intended meaning.
But this also creates risk.
If the listener identifies the wrong relevance, misunderstanding follows.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.10 The Role of Implication
Implication allows speakers to communicate more than they state.
For example:

“Some of the reports were accurate.”

This may imply:

Not all of the reports were accurate.

The speaker did not say this directly, but the listener may infer it.
Another example:

“He is very punctual.”

Said when discussing whether someone is qualified for a complex leadership role, this may imply that punctuality is the only positive thing the speaker can honestly mention.
The meaning comes not only from the sentence, but from what the sentence suggests in context.
Implication is powerful because it allows language to remain indirect while still carrying meaning.
But implication is also unstable.
Different listeners may infer different meanings.
This is why high-stakes communication often requires explicitness.
When misunderstanding would be costly, implication should be reduced.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.11 The Role of Tone
Tone changes meaning.
A sentence may have the same words but a different tone.

“I am fine.”

This may mean:

I am genuinely fine.
I am upset but do not want to discuss it.
I am angry and expect you to notice.
I am trying to end the conversation.
I am protecting myself.

Tone is not an optional layer.
It is part of how meaning is carried.
In written communication, tone may be signaled through:

word choice
punctuation
length
formality
response speed
formatting
emoji
hedging
directness
silence

For example:

“Okay.”

In one context, it may be neutral agreement.
In another, it may feel cold.
In another, it may signal resignation.
The sentence is minimal.
The tone carries much of the meaning.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.12 The Role of Silence
Meaning can also be produced by what is not said.
Silence is not always empty.
It may mean:

agreement
disagreement
hesitation
refusal
discomfort
lack of knowledge
respect
fear
resistance
strategic withholding

For example, in a meeting, if a manager proposes a plan and no one responds, the silence may have many possible meanings.
It may mean people agree.
It may mean they are confused.
It may mean they disagree but do not feel safe speaking.
It may mean the proposal is not important enough to discuss.
It may mean they need time to think.
The sentence is absent, but meaning is still present.
A pragmatic speaker must learn to interpret silence carefully.
Silence should not be overread, but it should not be ignored.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.13 Indirect Meaning
Indirect meaning is common in human communication.
People often use one form to perform another function.
A question may function as a request:

“Can you send me the file?”

A statement may function as a warning:

“The road is icy.”

A compliment may function as persuasion:

“You are good at explaining this.”

A description may function as criticism:

“The report is missing the conclusion.”

A suggestion may function as instruction:

“You might want to revise this section.”

Indirectness is not weakness.
It is a way of managing relationship, politeness, pressure, and social complexity.
